title: Why Parenting and Leadership Are the Same Skill Set (According to a Psychologist)
guestBio: Dr. Andrea Mata (Dr. Dre) is a clinical psychologist, national speaker, and founder of BrightSpot Families. She's the author of The Number Two Parenting Book and graduated summa cum laude from Valparaiso University with her doctorate from Kent State.
excerpt: A clinical psychologist reveals why parenting skills and leadership skills are identical, how gentle parenting went too far, and what authoritative parenting with high expectations actually looks like in both the boardroom and your living room.
metaDescription: Clinical psychologist Dr. Andrea Mata explains why parenting and leadership are the same skills, authoritative parenting vs gentle parenting.
keyTakeaways: Authoritative parenting combines high expectations with high support, creating the best outcomes for children and employees.
Gentle parenting swung too far from authoritarian into permissiveness, creating a generation struggling with adaptability.
It costs twice an employee's salary to fire and replace them, making coaching the smarter financial investment.
Gen Z employees need clear behavioral expectations and frequent check-ins, not vague annual reviews.
Seventy-one percent of household cognitive labor falls on mothers, creating an unsustainable mental load.
Motherhood is leadership training for every future relationship your child will have.
You don't have to be perfect as a parent or leader—you have to be present and consistent.

pullQuotes.text: Parenting your child IS modeling leadership for every future relationship
It costs 2x salary to fire and replace — coaching is the smarter investment
You don't have to be perfect — you have to be present

timestamps.label: The Biggest Challenge Facing Families and Workplaces
The Rise of Gentle Parenting and Where It Went Wrong
Parenting Skills and Leadership Skills Are the Same
Authoritative Parenting — High Expectations High Support
Gen Z in the Workplace — What Leaders Need to Know
The True Cost of Firing vs Coaching Employees

content:
What if I told you that the skills you use to parent your children are exactly the same ones you need to lead your team? According to Dr. Andrea Mata, a clinical psychologist and founder of BrightSpot Families, this isn't just a theory—it's backed by decades of psychological research and real-world application.

In this eye-opening conversation with Aimee Rickabus on The Manage Her Podcast, Dr. Mata breaks down why gentle parenting has swung too far into permissiveness, what authoritative parenting actually looks like, and how these principles translate directly into managing employees—especially Gen Z workers who are struggling with adaptability and resilience.

The Gentle Parenting Pendulum Swing

Let's address the elephant in the room: gentle parenting isn't working the way it was intended. Dr. Mata explains that while the movement started with good intentions—moving away from authoritarian, harsh parenting styles—it swung too far in the opposite direction, creating what she calls permissive parenting disguised as gentle parenting.

The result? A generation that struggles with discomfort, has difficulty adapting to challenges, and lacks the resilience needed to thrive in both personal and professional relationships. As Dr. Mata puts it, when we remove all friction from our children's lives, we're not preparing them for a world that will inevitably include friction.

This has created a ripple effect that extends far beyond the family home. Employers are seeing this play out in the workplace, where young employees struggle with feedback, have difficulty meeting expectations, and often lack the coping mechanisms needed to handle workplace stress and challenges.

Authoritative Parenting: The Sweet Spot

So what's the alternative? Dr. Mata advocates for authoritative parenting—a style that combines high expectations with high support. Think of it as high expectations wrapped in warm and fuzzies. This isn't about being harsh or demanding; it's about setting clear boundaries and expectations while providing the emotional support and guidance needed to meet them.

In authoritative parenting, you're not afraid to be the authority figure your child needs. You set expectations for behavior, follow through with consequences, and teach your children to tolerate discomfort and work through challenges. But you do all of this within a framework of love, support, and emotional availability.

The research is clear: children raised with authoritative parenting show better outcomes across every measure—academic achievement, emotional regulation, social skills, and long-term life satisfaction. They're more adaptable, more resilient, and better equipped to handle the challenges life throws at them.

Motherhood as Leadership Training

Here's where it gets really interesting: every interaction you have with your child is modeling leadership for every future relationship they'll have. When you set boundaries with love, follow through on consequences, and teach them to work through discomfort, you're not just parenting—you're teaching them how to be in relationship with authority figures, peers, and eventually, how to lead others themselves.

Dr. Mata makes a compelling case that motherhood is the ultimate leadership training ground. The skills you develop as a mother—setting expectations, providing feedback, motivating others, managing difficult conversations, and inspiring growth—are exactly the skills that make great leaders in any context.

This reframe is powerful for working mothers who might feel like they're juggling two completely different skill sets. You're not. You're developing and refining the same core competencies in different environments.

Applying Parenting Principles to Gen Z Management

The workplace applications are immediately obvious, especially when it comes to managing Gen Z employees. Dr. Mata explains that many young workers are entering the workforce without having learned to tolerate discomfort or adapt to challenging situations. They've been raised in environments where their feelings were always validated and their discomfort was immediately relieved.

As a leader, this means you need to approach Gen Z employees with the same authoritative style that works with children: high expectations combined with high support. Give clear behavioral expectations, provide frequent feedback and check-ins (think 12-week evaluations instead of annual reviews), and don't be afraid to hold them accountable while also providing the coaching and support they need to succeed.

The economic argument is compelling too. According to SHRM research that Dr. Mata cites, it costs approximately twice an employee's salary to fire and replace them. Coaching is always cheaper than firing, making the investment in developing your people not just the right thing to do, but the smart financial decision.

The Mental Load Reality

Of course, no conversation about motherhood and leadership would be complete without addressing the mental load. Dr. Mata confirms what many of us already know: research shows that 71% of household cognitive labor falls on mothers. This invisible work—remembering appointments, planning meals, tracking everyone's schedules, anticipating needs—is exhausting and often unrecognized.

The solution isn't to do everything yourself or to lower your standards. It's to recognize that this mental load is real work that deserves acknowledgment and support. It's okay to ask for help. It's necessary to delegate. And it's important to model for your children—and your employees—that leadership sometimes means knowing when and how to distribute responsibilities.

Permission to Be Imperfect

Perhaps most importantly, Dr. Mata gives us permission to abandon the Pinterest-perfect standard of motherhood and leadership. You don't have to be perfect—you have to be present. You don't need to have all the answers—you need to be willing to figure them out together.

This means embracing what Aimee calls audacious imperfection—showing up authentically, making mistakes, learning from them, and modeling resilience for both your children and your team. It means setting boundaries around screen time and technology not because you're a perfect parent, but because you understand the importance of teaching children to tolerate boredom and discomfort.

The bottom line is this: whether you're raising children or leading a team, the core skills remain the same. Set clear expectations, provide support and guidance, follow through with consequences, and create an environment where growth and learning can happen. Do this consistently, with love and firmness, and you'll develop humans—whether they're your children or your employees—who are equipped to thrive in an uncertain world.

Remember, you're not just managing your household or your team—you're developing the next generation of leaders, partners, and contributors to society. That's not just parenting or leadership; that's legacy work.

quiz.title: What's Your Leadership & Parenting Style?
quiz.description: Whether you're raising kids, managing teams, or both, discover how your natural approach to guidance and boundaries shows up in your relationships.

questions.question: When someone you care about is struggling with a challenge, your first instinct is to:

options.text: Listen to understand their feelings, then help them problem-solve with clear next steps
Offer comfort and reassurance that everything will work out
Share what worked for you in similar situations and encourage them to push through
Ask lots of questions to help them discover their own solution

questions.question: When you need to give difficult feedback, you typically:

options.text: Schedule a private conversation where you're honest but supportive about what needs to change
Wait for the right moment when they seem open, then frame it as gently as possible
Address it directly and clearly—they need to know where they stand
Start by asking for their perspective on how things are going

questions.question: Your philosophy about rules and boundaries is:

options.text: Clear expectations with room for individual circumstances and growth
Guidelines that can be flexible based on everyone's emotional needs
Consistent standards that apply fairly to everyone
Agreements we make together and can adjust as we learn

questions.question: When someone makes a mistake, you believe:

options.text: It's a learning opportunity that requires both accountability and support
The relationship is more important than perfect performance
Natural consequences teach important lessons
Everyone should have input on how to handle it moving forward

questions.question: In conflict situations, you tend to:

options.text: Address issues directly while staying emotionally connected to the person
Focus on preserving harmony and making sure everyone feels heard
Get straight to the facts and work toward a clear resolution
Facilitate discussion until we reach consensus together

questions.question: Your biggest concern when leading or guiding others is:

options.text: Finding the right balance between challenging them and supporting them
Making sure they feel loved and accepted no matter what
Ensuring they develop the skills and resilience they'll need long-term
Respecting their autonomy while still providing guidance when needed

results.title: The Supportive Guide
results.description: You naturally balance high expectations with warmth and understanding. You believe in people's potential and aren't afraid to challenge them while providing the support they need to succeed. Your strength lies in creating environments where others feel both pushed to grow and emotionally safe to make mistakes. This episode will validate your instincts and give you research-backed strategies to refine your authoritative approach even further.

results.title: The Gentle Nurturer
results.description: Your heart leads the way, and you prioritize emotional connection and harmony above all else. You're incredibly empathetic and want everyone to feel loved and accepted. Sometimes you worry that setting firm boundaries might damage relationships or hurt feelings. This episode will help you understand how adding structure and clear expectations to your natural warmth can actually strengthen your relationships and help others thrive.

results.title: The Standards Keeper
results.description: You have a clear vision of excellence and you're not afraid to hold yourself and others accountable to high standards. You believe that challenge builds character and that people rise to meet expectations. Your strength is in creating structure and driving results. This episode will show you how to maintain your high standards while adding the emotional support that makes people feel truly empowered to meet them.

results.title: The Flexible Collaborator
results.description: You believe in shared decision-making and value everyone's input equally. You're naturally democratic and want others to feel heard and respected in every situation. You sometimes struggle with when to step in and provide direction versus when to let others figure it out. This episode will help you understand when your collaborative approach serves others best and when clear leadership boundaries are actually the more loving choice.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Welcome back to The Manager podcast, the show where we make invisible labor visible, redefine leadership, and explore what it really means to thrive as women in today's world. Today's conversation is one I've been deeply looking forward to because it sits at the intersection of mental health, leadership, parenting, and the future of our culture.

I'm joined by Dr. Andrea D. Mata, Dr. Dre, a clinical psychologist, professor, national speaker, and one of the most compelling voices right now on Gen Z resilience and human development. She is also the author of the number two parenting book. Dr. Dre works with families, leaders, and organizations to help them understand what's really happening beneath the surface, why anxiety is rising, why burnout is normalized and how the way we parent, lead and set expectations is shaping the next generation.

She's bold, evidence-based, compassionate, and refreshingly honest. And today we're going to talk about everything from gentle parenting and accountability to Gen Z in the workplace and what women leaders need to understand right now about mental health, boundaries, and expectation. This is one of those conversations that will challenge you and also ground you. Let's get into it. Welcome to the show, Dr. Dre. I'm so glad to, let's do this.

**Andrea:** Hello, hello, thank you so much for having me. I'm excited.

**Aimee:** Yay. So Dr. Dre, you wear a lot of hats, clinical psychologist, professor, speaker, and parent. From your perspective, what is the biggest psychological challenge you're seeing right now across families, workplaces, and culture as a whole?

**Andrea:** I think what I'm seeing is the lack of adaptability. So when I talk about adaptability, I mean you have enough skills in your toolbox so that no matter what environment you go into, you can sit back, you can assess, and then you can figure out, okay, what needs to happen in order to thrive? And so that's what I talk about with adaptability, but there's so many of our youth who just feel so helpless and they've developed learned helplessness where they think everything is out of their control and they can't do anything better. And so they just kind of sit back and wait for everyone else to kind of do it for them. And I'm like, no, no, we're not going to do this. You have to start doing things yourselves for yourselves.

**Aimee:** And what did we learn from the way that those now adults were parented and how we can do better with parenting? What did that generation of parents do wrong with those kids?

**Andrea:** Yeah. So for the last 20 years, like I usually say 15 to 20 years, there's been a big influx and a big push for what I call gentle parenting by the influencers on Instagram and the supposed parenting experts who aren't really experts if you dive into their background and their training and all of the things.

And so what gentle parenting kind of emphasizes is it's going to emphasize feelings over thinking or behaviors. And the problem with that is that you don't have any control over your feelings. Then it's also going to get rid of punishments and rewards. And the reason why that's problematic is because why does the vast majority of Americans go to work? To get paid, not because of this internal motivation and this purpose and this passion. No, they go to their nine to fives because it pays them to be there.

And then the last thing is a lot with gentle parenting is they try to treat their child as if they are on the same level as them or like their partner. And so they're giving the kids a lot more power and decision-making ability than the child is actually ready for. And then what happens is when they get into the workplace, they're like, oh, well, I should, you should be listening to me. And the leaders are like, what? You just started yesterday. And they're like, yeah, but I should already be the boss. And the leaders are like, what? And so there's this whole weird dynamic going on between the Gen Zs and the millennials and the Xers and the boomers.

**Aimee:** That is so interesting. You don't really think about how parenting styles are going to affect workplace and businesses later on, but parenthood is leadership. We're teaching our kids leadership, whether we realize it or not in our homes.

**Andrea:** Yeah. And I think that's one of the biggest things, if I could scream it from the rooftops for corporations and businesses and companies is that parenting and leadership are literally the same skills. And so if you're, that's how you're modeling what a leader is by how you're parenting your child. And so then when they get to the real world and they get into the workplace, they're like, but this is not how my mom treated me. And then you're like, well, yeah, because I'm not your mom.

And so one of the things that I always talk with companies is like, well, why would we bring you a parenting expert, clinical psychologist into do trainings and workshops for us? And I was like, well, because if you bring me in, you get both parenting, which is building and investing in your employees personal side. And then you also get leadership training all in one workshop because parenting skills and leadership skills are literally the same thing. It's just different audiences. You're going to have a few kids that you're in charge of versus adults that you're in charge of, but it's literally the same skills.

**Aimee:** I love it. That's exactly what my book was all about. That's what I realized. I was like, whoa, this is exactly what I do at my house. Running this company is the same thing. This is wild. I wasn't expecting it. And when it hit me, the aha, I was like, wow, we should really be talking about this. This is pretty important stuff.

**Andrea:** Yeah. And what works in parenting works in leadership, especially now. And I think people are not really realizing it yet. But I do think there's a little bit of, I think the shift of consciousness seems to be happening.

**Aimee:** At least on my show, I'm seeing more and more people that are having kind of overlapping conversations in this area of parenthood is leadership. Motherhood is leadership. I think the message is spreading, Dr. Dre, and I'm glad because first of all, we need to give moms credit for this important work that they do at home.

And I think that's one of the things, one of the friends that I interviewed after I met with you is Dr. Danielle Dowling. And she wrote good girl, bad mom. And she really gets into the economics of how much it costs. It would cost if you had to pay somebody to be the stay at home mother. And then the policy that would go behind if we actually compensated women for their work, what that would look like if there was actually policy behind it and I'm like wow if we elevate motherhood to a position of leadership in our culture if we're able to put a policy behind it that actually puts a monetary value to motherhood it could really change the future for humanity for our people if we're able to do that in our culture that could be amazing

**Andrea:** Are you working? You're like, I'm working. I'm working. I did everything.

**Aimee:** Okay. All right, let me answer the question. Yes, yes. No, there really wasn't a question there. It's just, do you think that motherhood is something that our political leadership or that we as women should be lobbying for policy changes on so that women could be compensated to stay home and actually be mothers full-time to their children?

**Andrea:** So I think that's a really interesting conversation. And I don't know, I'm not strong in economics, but I know, I didn't read that book. I have to find it and read it. But I want to say that listening to Dave Ramsey, I want to say that he said it's like $95,000. If you take everything that a mom does, a stay-at-home mom does with grocery shopping and cleaning and cooking and laundry and all of the scheduling, personal assistance, those kinds of things. If you kind of figure all of that together, it's like $95,000 a year. But I don't know where, being me, it's like, where does that money come from? Maybe that you could get a tax credit or something.

I mean, I just, I think that if there could be some sort of offset or compensation or acknowledgement that there, that this has a financial, because unfortunately our world kind of defines success and potential and all of these things around money, because this is invisible labor that's unpaid. It has been diminished and kind of set aside and almost swept under the rug, but it's probably the most important work that happens for humanity. It has to be the most important work that happens for humanity. I believe that.

Absolutely. Because I know from my experience, both personally and professionally, that if you don't have a strong family unit, you are raising individuals who are more likely to break the law and not be contributing members of society. And then they're going to raise kids that are more likely to be abused both emotionally, psychologically, even sexually, those kinds of things. And it's just so problematic. And we're not having those conversations about how important the institution of family is and motherhood and fatherhood is like, that's a whole other epidemic that we're not talking about is the fatherlessness in our country.

But yeah, but the alternative is if you're raising your children in these loving environments, in these high quality families, well, then these are going to be the ones that are going to be successful. They're going to go out and they're going to make changes in the world. They're going to be great contributors to society and their communities and all of the good things that we want from our society. And if I'm doing it and then you're doing it, Aimee, and then our kids start to do it because that's what's been modeled for them. Well, then all of a sudden the ripple effects go exponential because all of these little ripple effects are just going all over the place. And then our society looks completely different if we prioritize family.

**Aimee:** So the ROI, the return on investment on motherhood, is very great for society. So it's something that our society should be investing in. Something that we need to put an emphasis on and an understanding as moms, as women, and come together and say, hey, look, if we can't do this work because we can't afford to stay home with our kids, if we wanted to stay home with our kids but we can't afford to stay home with our kids, is there a means, is there a way that we could look at some sort of policy that would allow women to do that if they wanted to?

I know that now we're actually paying siblings or children to care for their parents, like $75,000 a year to care for your parents because it's cheaper for the government and safer for you to care for your parent than it is for them to put them in a home. It's way more expensive, and the elder abuse was out of control and all of that stuff. So what I'm thinking is if some sort of policy like that became available for women, for women who basically couldn't afford to otherwise stay home with their children but would love to stay home with their children, because I think there are women like that out there. I'd like to stay home with my kids, but who can afford that? At least until they get into school, at least until they're school aged.

**Andrea:** Right. And that was one of the perks about being a college professor when my kids were young is that I was able to create my own schedule. So I didn't teach classes on Mondays and Fridays. I taught long days on Tuesdays and Thursdays and I taught a night class on Wednesdays. So my husband would come home from work and then I would go and be a professor. So I had the luxury of being kind of like, yeah, I was full time. But then because I had so much flexibility, I was still a stay-at-home mom. And there's some pluses and minuses of that.

But I think there are a lot of women out there who would love to have that ability to stay at home with their kids and probably do some co-op and meet with other moms who are staying home with their kids. And I think it would probably help with our society and with women's mental health. Because I know that there's been a research study, it came out probably in the last six to 12 months, and it was talking about how women's mental health, especially mothers, is awful because there's all of this pressure and responsibility on us and the burnout rates are just so high.

And I think about that. I'm like, if I only had to be a mom, if I did not have to be Dr. Dre and writing books and seeing clinicians and doing speaking events, if I only had to do a mom, I'm pretty sure my pressure levels, my stress levels would significantly decrease because I wasn't having to have all this fragmented time where it's like, okay, we were talking earlier today, fog delays, two hour delays for school. It's like, okay, now I have to maneuver all of my schedule around. But if I was just a mom and it was like, oh, okay, I'm going to take you guys to school two hours later, no biggie. And then we're to come home and then I'll fold the laundry and then I'll go grocery shopping and all of the things. I think my actual stress levels would actually be reduced if I was only quote unquote a mom.

**Aimee:** Yeah, I know. And then you put on top of that, you get women at a certain age and then the season of your life where your parents start aging. So you've still got children and now you have parents that need you as well. So there becomes this time in your life where you're the sandwich and the stress level. Now you're working. I remember my mom when my mom went through this, I really thought my mom was going to end up in a loony bin for about a year when her father got diagnosed with dementia. Her mother went blind in a week. And they ended up living with us while my mom was running her company and my brother and I were little kids. And it was just the pressure that can be put on a mother is, it isn't beneficial for society as a whole to have women under this much pressure.

**Andrea:** Not at all. I was having this very conversation with one of my clients earlier. And she's not even a mom yet. She wants to be a mom. She's in her late 20s, early 30s. And her parents are divorced. And so there's this weird kind of relationship where she feels responsible for both her mom and her dad. Her mom's physically healthy, but emotionally, mentally, not that great. But then her dad is not physically healthy.

And so we were having this conversation because she does a lot for her mom and she does a lot for her dad. And I looked at her and I was like, there's going to come, I hate that I have to tell you this, but it, and I'm going to, it's going to be a gut punch. And she's like, okay, I'm ready. She's like, give it to me. And I'm like, look, there's going to come a time where you have to choose between being a good mom and a good daughter. And she was like, oh, that's just, that's rough. And I was like, yeah, because you're going to have to choose between either or you're going to have to have conversations where you're going to have to be like, hey, I can't do all of this alone. I can't be the great mom and the great daughter and be sole responsibility for my dad's health and my mom's emotional well-being. You're going to have or you're going to have to call in your in-laws and other family members to help you be a great mom. And she's like, I'm like, you have a few years because you're not pregnant yet. But this is going to be something that's going to come up probably in the next five years for her.

**Aimee:** Yeah. It's a lot to be carrying both sides of the coin. And then our generation, I think there was a study that came out that said our generation has had the least amount of help from their parents in child rearing as well. So in past generations, there were grandparents who were around to help with the kids, help raise them, help mom, help dad, grandmas and grandpas. And I feel like us Gen Xers, we didn't really have as much of that. And do you think that that has impacted our burnout levels?

**Andrea:** Oh, absolutely. Because my dad, so when my parents found out that I was having twins, they were still in Chicago and then they're like, well, you're going to need help. And they're like, and we're retiring. They're like, so we're just going to move to you to help you. And I was like, and I literally will tell anybody, I can't do as much as I do, if I did not have my dad and my mom six minutes away, because earlier this week when there was the delays, it was like, I would just call my dad and be like, pa, they have a two hour delay, but I have clients. And he's like, just bring them over, mija. He's like, no biggie. And I was like, oh, thanks so much, pa.

But that's one of the things I love intergenerational care. There are so many benefits of intergenerational having grandparents taking care of their grandchildren as their adult children are going out in the workforce. I love it. I did years ago, I did a whole three part series on the benefits of it for the parents and the kids and the financial and the cognitive, all of the things.

And the thing is my kids and I were actually talking about this earlier this week because my husband and I's retirement goal, vision is that we will do the same thing for our kids so that we will, wherever our kids are, we will be there to help raise their kids so that they can go out and have their careers. And so the kids were funny. We were having breakfast one morning and the three kids were like, well, if we all have three kids, you and dad are going to have nine kids running around the house. And I was like, that's okay. We'll have nine kids running around the house. We'll just work with it. I'll just need to stay healthy, both mind and body, so that we can chase these nine kids around all over the house.

**Aimee:** I love it. That's my plan, too. Indy, I always ask Indy, my five-year-old, what do you want to be when you grow up? She's like, I just really want to be a mom. And I'm like, oh, she'll be like, what do you want to be? And I'm like, I really want to be a grandma. And she's like... And we talk about how we're going to live. We want to live together. My older boys will look at me and they'll be like, you're going to homeschool our kids, right? And I'm like, yeah, for sure. I'm going to homeschool your kids.

I'm looking forward to that season of my life and I'm cultivating the fields for the next season of my life so that we can have, I don't want to be a little old lady who lives in my house all by myself. I would be so lonely. I have a neighbor like that and I would, I'd be so lonely. I don't even know what I would do with myself.

**Andrea:** Right. Go and volunteer. But I'd much rather just be around my kids and raising their kids and hanging out and having nine kids running around the house. Sounds amazing. And we were joking because the kids were like, well, all the kids would just be around you. And dad would be the grandpa would be off reading in the corner and he'd just be all the kids around you. That's how it is now. And I was like, that's fine. I will play that role. I will love playing that role.

**Aimee:** Totally. You'll just have a really cool backyard, play structure for them.

**Andrea:** Exactly. And then I'll fill them up with all the sugar and then here you go. Here you go. Do the same thing my parents do to me where it's like TV time, sugar, ice cream, literally as soon as we go to pick up my kids from my parents house, they run to get those icies, those old school, in the frozen strips. And they just run and get, they're like, we have to get our thing. It's five degrees outside and they're still running to get their freaking icy pops. And I'm like, oh my gosh.

**Aimee:** That's hilarious. No, my grandparents were around before they got sick, my grandparents were totally around. They actually lived with us for a and it was so nice. I really, there was something there that was so beautiful and so special. And to be able to my dad says, because my parents were divorced, he says he's kind of been a tourist in my life. And when you come and you visit, but you don't really ever be together, but it was nice to actually get to spend that time with my grandparents where they weren't tourists. They were living in my house and I got to learn from them.

And I think that, man, I really think that, hashtag old people goals is hashtag grandma goals for sure. Just being a good grandma, I can't imagine missing any of it. I just hope I want to be a really, really, really good health so that we can grow up and be really, really awesome grandmas.

**Andrea:** Exactly. That's my goal. Because I'm doing this protocol right now to get in healthier, be healthier. And my son's like, why are you taking all these supplements? And I was like, so that I can, that's what's on the whole conversation. I was like, because I want to be healthy so that I can take care of your kids. And he was like, oh, and then this is how we had this whole conversation over breakfast.

**Aimee:** That is the cutest. Yeah. My kids see me doing it too. And I had the same thing. I'm like, well, I was quite old when I had Indy. I was 40 when I had her. So I'm like, I need to, I'm going to need to really take good care of myself. And she can't wait forever to have kids either because I won't be around forever. She needs to be 30 is about where 25. Maybe we won't have to push it up just a little because mommy was pretty old. Sorry. So you got to be the one.

**Andrea:** Yeah. We got to get into your book. Your book is called the number two parenting book. I know, but will you please tell the girls how you came up with the name for this book?

So the whole premise of the book is practical parenting tips and the time that it takes you to poop. A mom poop, not a dad poop, because those are very different times. Except if you're these two ladies in Lima, Ohio, who apparently take longer to poop than their husbands. And I was like, let me know what your secrets are, because I think you are the only two women in the universe who take longer than their husbands. And she's still like, yeah.

**Aimee:** That is hilarious. That is absolutely, a mom poop is, yeah, it's a mystical, magical, quick thing. Under five minutes.
